meta.title: 2.5 Checkpoint
meta.description: Trace data flow through a multi-component page and identify problems.

blocks.content:
## Checkpoint: The Data Flow

This checkpoint tests whether you can trace data through a React component tree, read conditional rendering, and spot common problems in list rendering. Work through these without scrolling back to the modules.

### Concept recall

blocks.prompt: Explain how props work in React. What is destructuring, and how do default values work? If a component shows the wrong data, describe how you would trace the value backward through the component tree to find the bug.
Describe the two conditional rendering patterns in React (ternary and &&). When do you use each one? What is the zero gotcha with && and how do you avoid it?
Explain the .map() pattern for rendering lists. Why does every item need a key prop? Why is using the array index as a key considered a bad practice?

blocks.content:
### Applied exercises

Read each code example and answer the question. These problems combine multiple Level 2 concepts.

options: A) Hello, Dana / Your plan: Pro
B) Hello, Dana Park / Your plan: Pro
C) Hello, Dana / Your plan: Free
problems.explanation: Trace the data: App passes user.firstName ('Dana') as the name prop and user.plan ('Pro') as the tier prop. UserGreeting displays {name} ('Dana') and {tier} ('Pro'). The default value 'Free' for tier is not used because App explicitly passes 'Pro'. Note that user.lastName ('Park') is never passed to UserGreeting, so only the first name appears.

options: A) The heading, then 'All caught up!' -- everything works correctly
B) The heading, then the text '0', then 'All caught up!' -- the && gotcha strikes
C) The heading only -- both conditional sections are hidden
problems.explanation:
Two things happen. First, {unreadMessages && ...} short-circuits because 0 is falsy, but React renders the number 0 as the text '0'. So the user sees a stray '0' on the page. Second, {unreadMessages === 0 ?

All caught up!

: null} correctly evaluates to 'All caught up!' because the condition is true. The fix for the first line: {unreadMessages > 0 && ...}.

options: A) Dashboard passes the prop as 'user' but Sidebar destructures it as 'username' -- the prop name does not match
B) Sidebar should use 'props.user' instead of destructuring
C) The currentUser object is missing a 'username' field
problems.explanation: Dashboard passes <Sidebar user={currentUser} />, which means the prop is named 'user'. But Sidebar destructures { username }, looking for a prop called 'username'. Since no prop called 'username' exists, it is undefined, and the ternary falls through to 'No user'. The fix: either change the parent to pass username={currentUser} or change the child to destructure { user } instead of { username }.

options: A) It renders three tag spans (React, JavaScript, Design) -- no problems
B) It renders three tag spans but the mapped elements are missing key props -- React will show a console warning
C) It renders 'No tags' because the condition is wrong
problems.explanation: The component correctly checks tags.length > 0 (true, since there are 3 tags) and maps over the array. Each tag object has an id field that should be used as the key. But the <span> inside .map() has no key prop. This will work visually but React will show a console warning, and the list may behave incorrectly if tags are added, removed, or reordered. The fix: <span key={tag.id} className='tag'>.

options: A) 'Your Order', a list of 2 items with prices, 'Discount applied!', and the total $21.48
B) 'Your Order', a list of 2 items with prices, and the total $21.48 (no discount message)
C) 'Your Order', an empty list, '0', and the total $0.00
problems.explanation: The heading 'Your Order' always renders. ItemList maps the 2 items into list items showing 'Notebook - $12.99' and 'Pen Set - $8.49'. The discount check: order.discount is 0, and {0 > 0} is false, so the discount message does not appear. Note: this uses > 0 (not the bare && pattern), so there is no zero gotcha here. OrderTotal receives the total (12.99 + 8.49 = 21.48) and displays 'Total: $21.48'.

blocks.content:
### Self-assessment

Rate your confidence on each Level 2 concept from 1 (cannot explain it) to 5 (could teach it):

| Concept | Confidence (1-5) |
| :-- | :-- |
| Reading props and destructuring | |
| Default prop values | |
| String props vs curly brace props | |
| One-way data flow (parent to child) | |
| Prop drilling | |
| Ternary conditional rendering | |
| && short-circuit rendering | |
| The zero gotcha | |
| .map() for list rendering | |
| The key prop and why index is bad | |

If any concept is below a 3, revisit that module before continuing to Level 3.

Level 2 fluency means you can trace data from where it is defined through the props it passes to the components that display it. You can read conditional rendering patterns (ternary and &&) and spot the zero gotcha. You can read .map() list rendering and know that missing or index-based keys are problems. If you can do all of this, you are ready for Level 3, where components start remembering things on their own with state.
